coredata.dc:title: Tuning optical properties of graphene quantum dots using photoexcited TiO2 for catalytic application
coredata.dc:creator: Muthurasu, A.
Ganesh, V.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple strategy to tune the emission property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from green to blue color using a photochemical method is proposed. Further, the resultant materials are demonstrated to be an efficient and effective catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol. Initially, green color emitting GQDs (g-GQDs) is prepared by using a chemical method involving acid reflux of graphene oxide obtained from a modified Hummer's method. Later these g-GQDs are exposed to photochemical excitation with titanium dioxide (TiO2) using UV light at a fixed wavelength of 365 nm and the resultant GQDs are observed to emit blue color (b-GQDs). Basically, the introduction of photochemically excited TiO2 nanoparticles provides excess electrons for g-GQDs to undergo further reduction to form b-GQDs leading to the formation of more sp2 hybridized domains within the carbon network than sp3 in the resultant b-GQDs. Consequently, the emission of GQDs is shifted from green to blue, and significantly ∼4 fold enhancement in the average quantum yield of GQDs is noted. These interesting research findings are analyzed by using zeta potential measurements, spectroscopic (FTIR, UV–visible, and Fluorescence) studies and microscopic investigation. Finally, TiO2 reduced GQDs are subsequently used for the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol, and the results obtained revealed that this material could potentially be employed as an effective catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catalyst
Charge carriers
Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Photochemical excitation
p-nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TiO2 nanoparticles and generation of photoexcited free electrons using TiO2
Application of b-GQDs as a catalyst for the conversion of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol
Electron transfer from photoexcited TiO2 nanoparticles to GQDs
Catalytic activity towards the reduction of p-nitrophenol

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: AM is thankful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) for pursuing a Ph.D. degree program under AcSIR. Authors also acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF) of CSIR – CECRI, Karaikudi for providing necessary equipments for characterization and analyses.

AM is thankful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) for pursuing a Ph.D. degree program under AcSIR. Authors also acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF) of CSIR – CECRI, Karaikudi for providing necessary equipments for characterization and analyses. CECRI Manuscript Communication Number: CECRI/PESVC/Pubs./2021–101

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographic images showing the color changes observed during a stepwise transfer of electrons. (1) Storing electrons in TiO2 by irradiating the deaerated ethanol suspension with UV light (365 nm) for 30 min (2) Injection of degassed g-GQDs until no blue color remains. This sequential addition leads to the formation of pink-colored solution due to the further reduction of functional groups on the surface of g-GQDs. Upon illumination with UV light, this solution emits blue color. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader isadvised to refer the web version of this article.)

UV–visible and PL spectra of the corresponding graphene quantum dots. (A) UV–visible absorption spectra of g-GQDs (a) and b-GQDs (b) respectively and PL spectra of g-GQDs (B) and b-GQDs (C) recorded at different excitation wavelengths. Inset: Photographic images of g-GQDs and b-GQDs in an aqueous solution taken under visible light and upon illumination with UV light (365 nm).
FTIR spectra of b-GQDs (a) and g-GQDs (b) respectively.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of (A) g-GQDs and (B) b-GQDs and the insets display the corresponding HRTEM images where the lattice planes of corresponding GQDs are clearly visible. The average particle size distribution of (C) g-GQDs and (D) b-GQDs are also displayed.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra recorded after the successive addition of NaBH4 to p-nitrophenol solution in the presence of b-GQDs. Inset shows the photographic images of p-nitrophenol before the addition of catalyst (b-GQDs). It exhibits green color and after the addition of catalytic amount of b-GQDs, the formation of colorless product is noted. (B, C) Control UV–Vis absorption spectra recorded after adding NaBH4 to p-nitrophenol solution in the presence of GQDs alone (B) and in the presence of TiO2 alone (C). (D) Plots of ln (At/A0) vs. time corresponding to the reduction of p-nitrophenol using b-GQDs (a), TiO2 (b) and g-GQDs (c) respectively. Inset shows the zoomed version of b and c plots. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is advised to refer the web version of this article.)
Pictorial representation depicting the conversion of g-GQDs into b-GQDs by using photoexcited TiO2 (e− & h+ pair).
Pictorial representation reveals the reduction of p-NP to p-AP using b-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Tuning optical properties of graphene quantum dots using photoexcited TiO2 for catalytic application

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Electrodics and Electrocatalysis (EEC) Division, CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI), Karaikudi, 630003, Tamilnadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR – Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR – CECRI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple strategy to tune the emission property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from green to blue color using a photochemical method is proposed. Further, the resultant materials are demonstrated to be an efficient and effective catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol. Initially, green color emitting GQDs (g-GQDs) is prepared by using a chemical method involving acid reflux of graphene oxide obtained from a modified Hummer's method. Later these g-GQDs are exposed to photochemical excitation with titanium dioxide (TiO2) using UV light at a fixed wavelength of 365 nm and the resultant GQDs are observed to emit blue color (b-GQDs). Basically, the introduction of photochemically excited TiO2 nanoparticles provides excess electrons for g-GQDs to undergo further reduction to form b-GQDs leading to the formation of more sp2 hybridized domains within the carbon network than sp3 in the resultant b-GQDs. Consequently, the emission of GQDs is shifted from green to blue, and significantly ∼4 fold enhancement in the average quantum yield of GQDs is noted. These interesting research findings are analyzed by using zeta potential measurements, spectroscopic (FTIR, UV–visible, and Fluorescence) studies and microscopic investigation. Finally, TiO2 reduced GQDs are subsequently used for the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol, and the results obtained revealed that this material could potentially be employed as an effective catalyst.

In this work, a simple strategy to tune the optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using photoexcited TiO2 is demonstrated by varying their emission color from green to intense blue. Upon UV light illumination, TiO2 nanoparticles lead to the formation of charge carriers (electron – hole pairs) that are exploited at interface of TiO2 – GQDs for tuning the emission property. Further this new concept and the resultant GQDs are utilized in the catalytic application for the selective reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is an interesting and unique material possessing an atom-thick two-dimensional honeycomb lattice structure [1]. Ever since its discovery in 2004, a great deal of attention has been paid from the viewpoints of both fundamental studies and applications. However, the zero band gap of graphene limits its application in many areas, especially in the fields of photoelectric and optoelectronics [2]. Thus, carbon-based fluorescent materials like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) which are derived from the carbonaceous materials evolved. Typically, GQDs are prepared from graphene oxide using modified Hummer's method, surface oxygen plasma treatment, one-pot electrochemistry and organic synthesis starting from small molecules like citric acid through pyrolysis [3–8]. Nowadays researchers focused mainly on tailoring the optical properties of GQDs through tuning of optical band gap by controlling the size, surface passivation, reduction of functional groups, and doping of heteroatoms like nitrogen and sulfur [9–12]. Therefore, the resultant GQDs possess some extraordinary properties such as large optical absorptivity, chemical stability, high photostability against photobleaching, blinking, biocompatibility and low toxicity [13,14]. Due to these superior properties, GQDs distinguish themselves from conventional fluorescent materials, making them an ideal candidate for exciting applications including bio-imaging [15], medical diagnosis [16], catalysis [17], and photovoltaic devices [18] etc. Though there are numerous preparation routes that have already been reported in literature, the solution-based scissoring method is one of the most widely used techniques to obtain large scale production and generates a wide variety of surface functional groups (oxygen containing functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups) on the edges as well as the basal plane. Moreover, these functional groups make them soluble in aqueous medium [19]. On the other hand, oxygen-containing functional groups introduce structural defects that can affect photoluminescence (PL) properties, especially in the case of carboxylic acid groups [20]. In order to improve the PL of GQDs several methods like surface grafting using polyethylene glycol (PEG), long-chain alkylamine [21,22], chemical reduction by sodium borohydride (NaBH4), thermal reduction with heating and photochemical reduction via isopropanol [23,24] are reported. However, these methods have some limitations especially the reduction of functional groups takes a longer time and needs some special kind of apparatus, in addition to involving toxic reagents. Moreover, these chemical modification processes by introducing different functional groups, induce severe structural defects on the surface of GQDs [23]. To overcome this problem, semiconducting metal oxide like TiO2 is proposed to be one of the best suitable charge carriers and under UV light illumination this leads to the formation of electron − hole pairs (e− & h+). This can be exploited in various processes occurring at the particle interface. Besides, the adsorptive affinity of TiO2 towards the organic compounds is very high; as a result, the oxidation and reduction processes occur more effectively on the surface of TiO2 [25]. Compared to conventional chemical reduction, the photochemical TiO2 reduction technique is environmentally benign. It can be easily regulated through exposure to light. It is extensively employed for the synthesis of metal nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide sheets [26,27]. To the best of our knowledge, there have been no reports on utilizing the photochemical TiO2 reduction method to enhance the PL of GQDs and subsequently tuning their emission properties.

Aromatic amines are widely used in chemical processes for example in the preparation of azo-dyes and in the biological field, some aromatic amine derivatives (6-aminochrysene) have been used as human dosimetry. Among them, p-aminophenol is an important amine and it has been used as industrial intermediates in the manufacturing of analgesic, antipyretic and hair drying agents [28]. Even if, there are several methods that have been employed for the preparation of aromatic amines but there are still many disadvantages such as catalyst recovery, cost, environmental concerns, safety, handling problems etc. [29–31] Several metal nanoparticles like Pd, Au, Ag, Pt and bimetallic compounds had been used for the catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol into p-aminophenol [29–37].

Keeping this in view, in the present work, emission property of GQDs is tuned from green color emission to blue color emission by using photoexcited TiO2 nanoparticles as a reducing agent. The charge separation of TiO2 nanoparticles under an inert atmosphere, assisted with UV light (365 nm) leads to the formation of electron – hole pairs. The majority of holes are occupied by excited electrons and the parts of holes are scavenged by ethanol molecules hence the remaining electrons are retained on the surface of TiO2 forming a blue color solution (Scheme 1 ). These accumulated electrons are used for the reduction of functional groups present on the surface of g-GQDs and consequently, the emission color is changed to blue resulting in the formation of b-GQDs. Usually, the reduction of g-GQDs is performed through chemical routes using NaBH4 and hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agents that are potentially hazardous and toxic in nature. Recently the reduction of g-GQDs by employing isopropanol as a reducing agent under the photochemical method is reported [24]; but it takes several hours, whereas the photochemical route reported here is one of the fastest and most reliable methods to reduce the functional groups present on GQDs with the aid of photoexcited TiO2 nanoparticles. As a result quantum yield associated with PL of GQDs is significantly enhanced and consequently the emission color also changed from green to blue. Finally, b-GQDs have been used as a potential catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol, and for comparison, similar catalytic activity is also investigated with g-GQDs and TiO2. Several kinetic parameters associated with the reduction process are also determined and compared with other reports.

Graphite powder, p-nitrophenol, and ethanol (99.9 % purity) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Bangalore, India. Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (95 % purity) was obtained from Alfa Aesar, Mumbai, India. Sulphuric acid and nitric acid were procured from Merck, Chennai, India. Millipore water having a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used for the preparation of all the aqueous solutions used in the analytical studies. All the other chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 nanoparticles and generation of photoexcited free electrons using TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to this experiment, the round bottom (RB) flask was thoroughly cleaned with a mixture of conc. H2SO4 and conc. HNO3; then washed several times with millipore water and then dried under N2 purging. In a typical experiment, about 10 mL of absolute ethanol was taken in RB flask and 1 mL of titanium isopropoxide was dropwise added into the absolute ethanol solution. The resulting mixture was vigorously stirred for 30 min; afterward the colloidal solution of TiO2 nanoparticles was formed. Further, TiO2 nanoparticles suspension was degassed using N2 for about 15 min. Then, this degassed suspension was exposed to UV light (365 nm) for about an hour leading to the formation of blue color solution which clearly indicates the generation of free electrons.

Graphene oxide was prepared according to modified Hummer's method by using the chemical oxidation of graphite powder. About 5 mg of as-prepared graphene oxide was cautiously dispersed into the mixture consisting of 8 mL of HNO3 (3.5 M), 2 mL of H2SO4 (1 M), and 20 mL of water. The reaction mixture was transferred into a 100 mL RB flask. Then the solution was heated at 250 °C by using an oil bath under refluxion for about 12 h. The obtained product was a brown-colored transparent clear solution. This resulting solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature. Afterward, the solution was ultrasonicated for about 30 min and then neutralized (pH −7) by adding sodium hydroxide solution. Finally, the same suspension was filtered through a 250 nm microporous membrane to remove the large-sized graphene oxide suspension and the filtrate was further dialyzed using the dialysis membrane bag for about 12 h (retain the molecular weight up to 12000 Da). This clear solution exhibits yellowish-green fluorescence while exposed to UV light and is represented as g-GQDs.

About 20 mL of as-prepared green color emitting graphene quantum dots (g-GQDs) was deaerated with N2 gas for about 15 min. Such a degassed solution was directly injected into 10 mL of blue-colored photoexcited TiO2 solution. During this period the blue color solution slowly changed into pale pink colored colloidal suspension. The formation of pink color indicates that some of the electrons are still present within the graphitic network of GQDs. Finally, the pink color slowly disappeared in presence of molecular oxygen and turns into a pale yellow color. The resulting solution was illuminated with UV light (365 nm) and it was observed to emit blue color fluorescence. It is labeled as b-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of b-GQDs as a catalyst for the conversion of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the catalytic activity of b-GQDs towards the reduction of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol was investigated using a quartz cuvette in the presence of photoexcited TiO2 reduced b-GQDs and the aqueous solution of sodium borohydride. In a typical experiment, an aliquot of 50 μL of b-GQDs solution (for the preparation of catalytic doses 5 mg of b-GQDs was dispersed in 10 mL of water) was added into 3 mL of p-nitrophenol (1 × 10−4 M) and then a freshly prepared aqueous solution of 100 μL of NaBH4 (5 × 10−3 M) was added. The photocatalytic reduction reaction was investigated using a commercial UV reactor furnished with 14.8 W ultraviolet lamps. The catalytic reduction reaction was monitored by measuring the absorbance at a fixed wavelength of 400 nm over different time intervals by using UV–visible spectrophotometer. The absorbance values were measured at different stages of the reaction. The product formation was analyzed and confirmed by using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and mass spectrometry.

Absorbance and fluorescence spectra were recorded using UV−VIS PerkinElmer Lamda 650 and Infinite M200MPC model. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained from TECNAI G2 20 FEI model operated at 200 kW. TEM samples were prepared by drop-casting method onto a copper grid. SEM images of the resultant materials were analysed using Hitachi SEM model S3000–H. FTIR spectra were recorded using Bruker Optic GmbH TENSUR 27 model operated with 162 software Opus version 6.5 m. Zeta potential values were measured using Bechmann Coulter Desla™ Nano C model purchased from USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transfer from photoexcited TiO2 nanoparticles to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Semiconducting TiO2 nanoparticles undergo charge separation and create electron – hole pairs while illumination under UV light (>365 nm) in absolute ethanol. Majority of the excited electrons comes back to recombine with the holes, and only a part of the holes are scavenged by ethanol molecules [38]. Hence, the remaining excited electrons turn into surface traps creating defects at Ti4+ sites. These trapped electrons are stable for many days if the system is kept free from O2, which acts as an electron scavenger [39]. In this work, initially we prepared g-GQDs, and subsequently, as-prepared g-GQDs solution was deaerated with nitrogen gas for 15 min and then injected into photochemically excited TiO2 solution creating charge carriers; suddenly the blue color solution turned into a pale pink color solution. Interestingly, the pink colored solution is found to be stable for a very long time under inert atmosphere conditions. UV–visible absorption spectrum of the resultant pink color solution shows a maximum absorption peak at 475 nm. On the other hand, as-prepared TiO2 nanoparticles did not show any absorption peak (Electronic Supporting Information [ESI]; Fig. S1), suggesting the excess of electrons are stored within the carbon network of GQDs [38]. But, in the presence of O2 atmosphere, the radical scavenger, the excess electrons are absorbed and as a result, pink color is slowly changed into pale yellow color. Finally, when the resultant solution was exposed to UV light, it emits a bright blue color as shown in Fig. 1 . This observation clearly reveals the stepwise transformation of emission color of GQDs. Hence, based on the observation and in agreement with the literature, a possible charge transfer mechanism between TiO2 and GQDs is proposed as follows [38,39].
(1)
TiO 2
+ h ʋ →
TiO
2
(
h +
e )
→
C 2
H 5
OH
TiO 2
(
e −
)
+
C
·
2
H 4
OH +
H +
(2)
TiO 2
( e )
+ g GQDs
→
TiO
2
+ reduced
GQDs
(
pink
color
intermediate
)
(3)
Reduced
GQDs
( pink
color
intermediate )
+
O
2
→
b ‐ GQDs
( pale yellow color )

TiO2 is a well-known material in the field of photocatalytic applications exhibiting many interesting and exciting properties. Here, TiO2 nanoparticles are prepared through in situ method by illumination with UV light (365 nm) under inert conditions. Moreover, exposure to UV light also leads to the charge separation. Majority of the charge carriers are recombined and a part of holes are occupied by ethanol to generate ethoxy radical (reaction 1) and the excess of electrons are accumulated on the surface defects of Ti4+ site. These accumulated electrons interact with deaerated g-GQDs; the portion of electrons are transferred to GQDs leading to the reduction of functional groups (reaction 2). Finally, in presence of molecular O2, the pink color intermediate solution slowly changed into pale yellow color solution which upon illumination with UV light emits blue color leading to the formation of b-GQDs (reaction 3).

UV–visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic studies are useful to find out the optical properties of g-GQDs and b-GQDs. Fig. 2 A shows UV–visible spectra of g-GQDs (a), and b-GQDs (b) respectively. In the case of g-GQDs (a), the absorption spectrum exhibits two absorbance peaks at 350 nm and 450 nm. These peaks are mainly originated from π-electron transition within the graphitic network and the oxygen-containing GQDs on the basal plane. The peak at 350 nm is designated to π-π* transition of CC, and another absorption peak noted at 450 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO bond. Similarly, after the reduction of g-GQDs with the use of TiO2 (e−), the absorbance peak at 450 nm (n–π*) disappears and only a single peak is observed at 350 nm (π–π*) (b), which indicates that most of the carboxylic and epoxide functional groups are effectively reduced in presence of excess of electrons resulting in the reduction of majority of the functional groups (n–π*) on the surface of GQDs. Fig. 2B and C display the photoluminescence spectra (PL) of g-GQDs (2B) and b-GQDs (2C) respectively. These spectra were recorded by changing excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 450 nm and the resultant data are analyzed. From Fig. 2B it is noted that maximum emission is observed at 525 nm for excitation at 360 nm for g-GQDs. Likewise, b-GQDs exhibit maximum emission at 405 nm for an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The excitation and emission spectra of both g-GQDs and b-GQDs are shown in Fig. S2, ESI. Interestingly, the emission wavelengths associated with both these GQDs are shifted depending upon the excitation wavelength. In case of g-GQDs, the noted shift is appreciable when compared to b-GQDs. In general, for the case of GQDs, the distribution of particle size plays a vital role in PL property and this can contribute to the observed differences in emission wavelengths. A wide size distribution might result in a variety of emission peaks. The surface chemistry of GQDs, including functional groups (-COOH and –CO) and defects, can alter their electronic structure. Different surface alterations can result in a wide range of emission properties. The excitation wavelengths employed during PL measurements have the potential to affect the corresponding emission spectra. Excitation energies that differ can selectively excite distinct electronic states, resulting in varied emission wavelengths. Accordingly, g-GQDs exhibit significant shift on comparison to b-GQDs due to the presence of several functional groups, as stated earlier. The average quantum yield values of these GQDs are calculated by using quinine sulfate as a standard and the quantum yield of g-GQDs and b-GQDs are found to be 2.6 % and 9.5 % respectively (S3, ESI). These results clearly indicate the photochemical reduction improved the quantum yield of GQDs. Green emission is attributed to the defect state emission because as-prepared green color emission GQDs consist of both sp2 and sp3 domains which create a series of energy traps within GQDs. Blue emission consists of the reduction of functional groups (-COOH and CO) with excess of electrons, and remove the majority of non-irradiative recombination functional groups, especially carbonyl groups that are converted into alcohol. These functional groups (-OH) are electron-releasing groups which induce the irradiative recombination process. Thus, all the surface states of b-GQDs are mostly disappeared and hence the emission is assumed to take place only through the radiative transition of electronic conjugation of sp2 carbons (perhaps π–π* transition). Therefore the resulting spectrum is shifted to blue region [19,38].

In addition, the presence of functional groups on the surface of GQDs can be analyzed by the most widely used technique like Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The surface functional groups on both the GQDs are analyzed. Fig. 3 shows FTIR spectra of g-GQDs (b) and b-GQDs (a). Strong stretching vibration of –C-OH occurs at 3445 cm−1 and CO stretching is observed at 1629 cm−1 for both g-GQDs and b-GQDs respectively. Vibration bands at 620 cm−1 for g-GQDs and 750 cm−1 for b-GQDs are due to the aromaticity of CC bond. However, the peak intensity of b-GQDs is much weaker than g-GQDs, mostly in the case of CO and –OH functional groups' peak intensity. This is because the excited TiO2 (e−) is a strong reducing agent and it reduced most of the functional groups on GQDs surface and some of the groups are cleaved from the surface.

Further zeta potential measurement was also carried out to assess the surface charge of the GQDs. Basically, zeta potential measures the electrostatic force of repulsion between the charged species in a solution. In case of GQDs, the presence of functional groups on their surface plays a crucial role in determining the overall surface charge [40]. More specifically, polar functional groups such as carboxyl groups (-COOH) and carbonyl groups (-CO), which contain oxygen atoms, contribute to the generation of negative charges on the surface. Thus, the reduction of g-GQDs into b-GQDs is confirmed by zeta potential (ζ) measurement too. This method has been used to find out the stability and surface charge of the resultant material. The zeta potential (ζ) of as-prepared g-GQDs is found to be −13.44 mV and for b-GQDs it is measured to be −5.5 mV. These results suggest that initially, g-GQDs contain a large number of carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxide groups predominantly present on the aromatic edges and basal plane of GQDs. The presence of these functional groups on the surface of GQDs induces negative charges. When these functional groups are converted into hydroxyl (-OH) groups or removed from the surface of GQDs, the total negative charge on the GQDs may diminish or even switch to positive value depending upon the nature of functional groups present on their surface. Consequently, a reduction in the number of negatively charged groups lead to decrease in the magnitude of the zeta potential (ζ) value, as observed in the case of b-GQDs. These results are consistent with FTIR results discussed earlier.

Particle size and structural morphology of the resultant GQDs are examined by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopic (TEM) studies. Fig. 4 displays TEM images of the GQDs studied in this work and these images reveal the distribution of GQDs with varied particle size. Additionally, the corresponding HRTEM observations are shown as insets of Fig. 4A–B that demonstrate the formation of crystalline phase of GQDs. The interplanar spacing is calculated to be ∼0.21 nm corresponding to (100) lattice planes of the graphitic carbon [11]. Furthermore, the particle size distribution of these GQDs was also analyzed and shown inFig. 4C–D respectively for g-GQDs and b-GQDs and the corresponding average particle size of g-GQDs is determined to be 5.5 nm (Fig. 4C) and for b-GQDs, it is measured to be 2.5 nm (Fig. 4D). Moreover, the crude solution of TiO2 reduced b-GQDs is also analyzed under the dried condition using SEM and the corresponding image is shown in Fig. S4, ESI. It can be noted that b-GQDs are uniformly distributed over the TiO2 nanoparticles surface. Moreover, the EDX analysis and elemental mapping studies indicate the presence of Ti, C, and O elements in the resultant material (Fig. S5, ESI), correlating with the proposed mechanism associated with the formation of b-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity towards the reduction of p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol is carried out by using mostly precious metals like Au, Pt, Ag, and Pd that have been well-reported in the literature [41]. But, very few works reported the catalytic reduction of p-nitrophenol with carbon-based fluorescent materials [42]. In order to evaluate the catalytic properties of photoexcited TiO2 (e−) reduced graphene quantum dots (b-GQDs), the reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) to p-aminophenol (p-AP) is investigated. UV–visible spectroscopy is a powerful technique to monitor the catalytic reduction of p-NP at various time intervals and follow the kinetics of reduction. Initially, the characteristic absorbance spectrum of p-NP shows a peak at 316 nm, and with the addition of freshly prepared cold NaBH4, the color of p-NP solution turns into a pale yellow to deep yellow color, as a result the absorption peak is shifted to 400 nm. This is because the addition of NaBH4 increases the alkalinity of the solution, and hence phenol under the alkaline condition is converted into phenolate ions, leading to the red shift at 400 nm in the spectrum (Fig. S6, ESI) [41]. In the absence of catalyst, the aqueous solution of p-NP and NaBH4, did not show any appreciable reduction and the reaction did not proceed with time. In general, the reduction reaction of p-NP is a thermodynamically allowed process and the thermodynamic potential, E o for 4-NP/4-AP = −0.76 V and H3BO3/BH4 − = −1.33 V vs. NHE favours the process. But it is kinetically sluggish in the absence of a catalyst [41,42]. However, while the catalytic amount of TiO2 reduced GQDs is added to the reaction mixture, the characteristic peak at 400 nm which is ascribed to the nitro compounds, is gradually decreased and simultaneously a new peak is observed at 314 nm, which clearly proved the formation of p-AP [43]. The complete conversion of the reaction is also confirmed by the clear color change from bright yellow color solution to colorless solution and the absorption peak of a nitro compound is no longer been observed, denoting the complete catalytic reduction of p-NP. Moreover, the reaction is completed within 5 min and the UV–visible spectrum exactly matches with pure aminophenol (Fig. S7, ESI). Fig. 5 A shows time-dependent p-NP reduction by NaBH4 in the presence of b-GQDs. This reduction process follows pseudo-first-order kinetics since the concentration of NaBH4 exceeds than p-NP; the rate of reduction is independent of the concentration of NaBH4 [42].

Furthermore, it is essential to point out that either TiO2 (or) GQDs alone do not have any catalytic activity under the testing environment and the corresponding UV–visible spectral data recorded for the reduction of 4-NP in NaBH4 using these materials are shown in Fig. 5B & C. It is revealed that the UV–Vis absorption spectra of pure TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 5C) and g-GQDs (Fig. 5B) in the reaction mixtures of p-NP and NaBH4 are also unaltered with respect to time. These results clearly point out that the pure TiO2 nanoparticles did not contain any catalytic activity towards p-NP reduction. However, in the case of g-GQDs (Fig. 5B) a feeble reduction of p-NP is noticed, but it is not effective even after 3 h of reduction. Hence, it is proved that only the addition of TiO2 (e−) along with reduced GQDs (b-GQDs) results in activating the catalytic reaction by transmitting electrons from BH4 − donor to the acceptor molecules of p-NP, and the reduction reaction proceeds. This clearly indicates that the combination of TiO2 (e−) and reduced GQDs (b-GQDs) is more catalytically active than a single component consisting of either pure TiO2 (or) g-GQDs alone. Fig. 5D shows the logarithmic plot of (At/A0) against the time and the apparent rate constant is calculated using the following equation [32].
(4)
[
A t
]
=
[
A 0
]
e
‐ kt
‐ ‐ ‐ ‐ ‐
where At is the absorbance at given time t, A0 is the absorbance at the initial stage, i.e. initial concentration of the reactant and k is the activity factor representing the pseudo-first-order rate constant. The apparent rate constant, K depends upon the amount of catalyst used in the p-NP reduction reaction and is determined to be 1.87 × 10−1 s−1 for the reduction of p-NP using b-GQDs. Moreover, the activity factor, k is calculated by dividing the rate constant, K with the total weight of the catalyst (m), using the relation, k = K/m. The performance of various catalysts studied in this work is compared with other reported catalysts and the comparison table is shown in ESI (S8). The activity factor of TiO2 reduced GQDs is 37.4 s−1g−1. This higher value of b-GQDs arises from the facilitated mass transfer with enhanced graphene structure. Hence, increased sp2 hybridized domains, leading to enhancement in the conductivity of GQDs. This affords a high concentration of p-NP to be adsorbed on the graphene network via π−π stacking interaction and hence the increased electron transfer from TiO2 nanoparticles to GQDs. This could lead to significant increase in the concentration of localized electrons and assists the uptake of electrons by p-NP. Moreover, TiO2 network also provides a favorable pathway for hot electrons to be delivered to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of p-NP. The proposed mechanism is depicted in Scheme 2 . When TiO2 was exposed to UV light, the electrons from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 are excited to the conduction band (CB) and eventually transferred to b-GQDs, which may then be pumped into the LUMO of p-NP [37]. Similarly, photoexcited GQDs have the ability to accept electrons. Photoexcitation of GQDs is caused by the absorption of plasmon into the graphene-like structure that can quickly separate the photogenerated electrons from holes that are trapped in their surface defect sites [43]. The photoexcited TiO2 electrons adsorbed near a hole will transfer electrons to the b-GQDs and facilitate the dissociation of H2O to produce the active protons (H+) because the hydrogen atoms adsorbed by BH4 − can connect with hydroxide ions to produce H2O or combine to generate H2. These water-derived active H+ species, combined with photogenerated electrons, are crucial for the reduction of adsorbed p-NP molecules to p-AP. Based on the above, it is reasonable to state that the strong, synergistic interactions between TiO2 and b-GQDs considerably improve the catalytic activity of p-NP reduction under UV–visible light irradiation compared to the reaction performed in the dark [8,43,44].

In addition, the formation of p-AP is also confirmed by using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis (Fig. S9, ESI). The formation of p-AP was monitored during the specified interval time and it can be noticed that the peak corresponding to p-NP gradually decreases, and a new peak corresponding to p-AP is formed as evidenced from the intense peak appeared at an eultion time of 3.1 min. Furthermore, gas chromatography – mass spectroscopic (GC-MS) studies are also carried out (Fig. S10, ESI). The appearance of a peak at m/z 105.6 suggests the formation of a product, p-AP. Based on HPLC and GCMS studies, the conversion of p-NP to p-AP is confirmed and the reaction proceeds with the addition of catalytic amount of b-GQDs. Overall, these results clearly demonstrate the optical tunability of GQDs with the help of photoexcited TiO2 and its subsequent application in the field of catalysis.

In summary, a successful tuning of the optical properties of g-GQDs is demonstrated by employing TiO2 nanoparticles as a reducing agent. Photochemical reduction is explored by extracting the excess electrons generated from TiO2 during illumination under UV light, because it is a well known charge carrier. After the reduction, PL of GQDs is blue shifted and the quantum yield is significantly enahnced. Interestingly, the reduction time of this particular method is found to be very short and increases sp2 domains which lead to appreciable enhancement in the photoluminescence properties of GQDs. TiO2 reduced b-GQDs are demonstrated to possess a high adsorption ability towards organic compounds leading to effective electron transfer from GQDs to TiO2 nanoparticles, makes it an excellent catalyst for the reduction of p-NP to p-AP.

A. Muthurasu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. V. Ganesh: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AM is thankful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF) for pursuing a Ph.D. degree program under AcSIR. Authors also acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF) of CSIR – CECRI, Karaikudi for providing necessary equipments for characterization and analyses.

Interfac. 6 (2014) 4883-4890. https://doi.org/10.1021/am4059183.
